title: BLT sandwich
subtitle: Crisp streaky bacon, ripe tomato, and lettuce with mayonnaise on toasted white
excerpt: Bacon, lettuce, and tomato: three ingredients that improve each other on toasted white bread with a generous spread of mayonnaise. The tomato should be ripe and at room temperature, the bacon properly crisp, and the toast warm when you assemble. Nothing complicated.
sourceNotes: The BLT became widespread in American diners after refrigeration made crisp iceberg lettuce and refrigerated bacon year-round staples by the 1940s and 1950s. The combination of bacon, lettuce, and tomato appears in club sandwich recipes from the early twentieth century; the three-ingredient BLT as a named sandwich solidified in mid-century American diner culture. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) documents the American sandwich and bacon traditions that underpin it.

content.text: The BLT is held together by the contrast of temperatures and textures: hot crisp bacon, cold lettuce, cool sliced tomato. The mayonnaise is not a condiment; it is the structural element that keeps the tomato from sliding and gives the sandwich its richness. There are only three variables to get right: the bacon should be properly crisp (not chewy, not brittle), the tomato should be ripe and at room temperature rather than cold from the fridge, and the toast should be warm when you assemble.

Streaky bacon gives a better crunch than back bacon and renders down to the right size to fit the bread. Cook it in a dry pan until the fat turns glassy and then crisps. Do not rush this step; undercooked bacon is the most common failure.

Lay the streaky bacon in a cold frying pan in a single layer. Set over medium heat and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until the fat is rendered and the bacon is deeply browned and properly crisp. Transfer to a plate lined with kitchen paper to drain. The pan should be dry; streaky bacon renders enough fat to cook itself.

Toast the bread while the bacon drains. Spread a generous layer of {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise on all four slices. Season the tomato slices with a little salt and pepper. On two slices of toast, layer the lettuce, then the tomato, then the hot bacon. Press the second slice of toast down firmly, cut in half on the diagonal, and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Bacon is chewy rather than crisp
items.cause: Cooked at too high a temperature or for too short a time
items.fix: Start in a cold pan over medium heat; the fat needs time to render before the bacon crisps — total cooking time is typically 6 to 8 minutes

items.cause: Tomatoes added while still wet from washing, or sandwich assembled too far in advance
items.fix: Dry the tomato slices with kitchen paper before assembling; eat the sandwich within a few minutes of making it

items.cause: Toast cut immediately rather than after a brief rest
items.fix: Let the assembled sandwich sit for 30 seconds before cutting; this allows the mayonnaise to settle and prevents tearing

items.cause: Tomato insufficiently seasoned or too cold
items.fix: Season the tomato with salt and pepper directly; tomato from the fridge has less flavour than one at room temperature

content.text: The BLT is the simplest of the American sandwich canon, which is also why it endures. It requires nothing beyond what most households have at any point during the year once you have access to year-round refrigeration — which is why the sandwich only became ubiquitous after the 1940s, when widespread refrigeration made both bacon and lettuce permanent kitchen staples. The combination has a short American history but feels as though it has always existed, because the flavours are so obviously right together. Nothing about it needs improving.
